rank_note: Rank intentionally null. site/src/data/indexes/us-states.json contains only 21 of 51 jurisdictions; ranks 9-38 were lost in the original legacy-HTML extraction and survivors were renumbered contiguously, so the displayed rank column is not national rank. Wisconsin must not be assigned a rank until the index is rebuilt with all 51 jurisdictions.
calibration_note: Not calibrated to published us-states rank order (known corrupt). Calibrated to methodology anchors and to the states assessed at full depth on 2026-07-19: Georgia 26.9, Iowa 30.6, Arizona 36.2, Ohio 38.1, Alaska 47.5, Colorado 52.5, Michigan 53.8, Delaware 53.8, Pennsylvania 54.4, New York 56.9, Illinois 60.0. Wisconsin at 53.1 sits between Colorado and Michigan/Delaware. Composite verified with computeCompositeFromDimensions in site/scripts/lib/scoring.mjs. Verification output: {"composite":53.1,"band":"Functional","integrationPremium":0}. Base composite 53.125; stdDev across the 8 dimensions 0.2634 (consistencyMult 1.0); 8 of 8 dimensions below 4.0 so weaknessFactor 0 and integrationPremium 0. Sensitivity cases, each run through the same function: EQU lowered to 3.2 gives {"composite":52.5,"band":"Functional","integrationPremium":0}; SYS lowered to 3.4 gives {"composite":52.5,"band":"Functional","integrationPremium":0}; both lowered gives {"composite":51.9,"band":"Functional","integrationPremium":0}. The Functional band is stable across the plausible scoring range.
attribution_note: Scores Wisconsin state conduct only. Medicaid expansion is an optional state choice under the Affordable Care Act, so the Joint Finance Committee's May 2025 rejection is fully scored; the mitigating fact that Wisconsin covers BadgerCare to 100 percent of the federal poverty level and is the only non-expansion state without a coverage gap is also scored. Federal cuts to homelessness and CDC lead-screening funding are context and are not scored against the state, but the state's own decision to strike the proposed $24M homelessness increase from 2025 Act 15 is scored. The Lincoln Hills and Copper Lake consent decree was federally imposed, so completion of all 50 reforms is scored as correction under external pressure (AB2 = 3) rather than self-initiated correction. Opioid settlement money arises from national litigation, but Wisconsin controls allocation and pace, so unspent balances and the April 2026 DHS $31M plan are scored. The Milwaukee Public Schools lead crisis was primarily a city and district matter; only the state DHS noncompliance order of 2025-03-19 and state screening support are scored. The July 2025 Wisconsin Supreme Court abortion ruling is judicial, not executive or legislative, and is used only as context for B5.
key_evidence: Governor Evers signed 2025 Wisconsin Acts 200 and 201 on 2026-04-06, releasing roughly $125M in PFAS funds the Joint Finance Committee had withheld since 2023 - about $80M for local remediation grants and $35M for well compensation. The correction took a 2024 veto and about three years of delay.

Wisconsin imprisons 1 in 36 Black adults, the highest rate of any state. Black residents are 6 percent of the population and 42 percent of the prison population, imprisoned at nearly 12 times the white rate against a national figure near 5 times. The disparity is measured and has not narrowed.

A federal judge ended oversight of Lincoln Hills and Copper Lake on 2026-01-29 after independent monitors verified all 50 court-ordered reforms. The same facilities were required by 2018 Act 185 to close by 2021 and remained open with no firm closing date as of June 2026.

Legislative Audit Bureau Report 26-03 (2026-03-20) disclosed 3 material weaknesses, 17 significant deficiencies, $2.6M in questioned costs, 48 recommendations and 8 repeat findings that the Bureau said show agencies did not act on prior recommendations.

Wisconsin legislators remain exempt from the record-retention part of the open records law and may delete emails. The governor's 2025 budget fix was stripped by the Joint Finance Committee and the loophole was still open in March 2026.

2025 Act 15 raised special education reimbursement from 30 percent to 42 percent in FY26 and 45 percent in FY27 on a bipartisan vote, while the DHS Children's Long-Term Support waitlist fell from about 2,600 children waiting three years in 2017 to under 1,000 waiting about 70 days.

evidence.claim: independently verified multi-year correction; scored as court-compelled rather than self-initiated

evidence.quote: Wisconsin state legislators have exempted themselves from the record retention portion of the state's open records law
